text: City Hall publishes everything. Nobody reads it. The vote happens — you find out after. I know: I sit on Milwaukee's City Plan Commission. This spring a data center was headed for a vacant Walmart, right next to homes — and the city's filing never called it that. It called it a "computational research facility." The neighborhood decoded it anyway, packed a seven-hour hearing — and the developer dropped it. They won. But they did it in the dark. Gavel is so the next block doesn't have to.

This is not a chatbot — nobody asked it anything. Gavel watches the city's agenda system; I'm firing this alert manually so it lands on camera — in production it's a five-minute cron. Plain English and Spanish, because that's who lives here. What it is, why it matters, how to be heard — and the local reporting, right on the card: "redevelopment drops data center plan."

And it knows what this neighborhood already said — in their own words — next to the city's official filing. These neighbor messages are real documented sentiment, posted fresh, because Slack can't backdate. Gavel queries them live through Slack's Real-Time Search API — and never stores them. Your neighbors' conversations are not a training set. That's why this is safe in a real community's Slack.
Residents asked for months: who is actually behind this? Nobody would say. Gavel pulls the property record live. AFS Milwaukee, L.L.C.

Then there's what they called it. Milwaukee publishes video of these meetings — not transcripts. What gets said in that room is, in practice, unsearchable. Gavel transcribed the hearing and found the moment — watch it search. It clipped it out of the webcast too; I posted that clip before recording — in production you get a timestamped link, which is exactly what it just did.
A "computational research facility." You cannot fight a thing that nobody will call by its name. Gavel doesn't need you to know the name.

They beat the data center on their block. But on July twentieth, the city writes the rules for data centers everywhere in Milwaukee — file two six zero one four two — on the same agenda as this site. That vote is still coming. Most of the city has no idea.

Knowing isn't enough — you have to answer back. A neighbor asks in Spanish; Gavel answers in Spanish — not translated, written in Spanish. Then it drafts her public comment — her words, her position — ready to file before the hearing. She edits it. She sends it. In this demo that goes to a test inbox, never a real city clerk. A human is always in the loop. Information becomes action.

Three memories, one agent. The official record — a custom MCP server over Milwaukee's Legistar and property data. The public spoken record — transcripts and zoning code. And the community's own memory — live through Slack's Real-Time Search, never stored. Take that away and Gavel goes deaf to its neighborhood, or starts warehousing people's messages. It's why the architecture is shaped like this.

That neighborhood won because a handful of people decoded a "computational research facility" — and showed up. Most blocks find out after. Milwaukee runs on Legistar — so do three hundred other cities — and the Milwaukee Civic MCP server is open source today. Gavel is how they find out before.
